heroOverview: Rabies is a viral encephalitis caused by lyssavirus, transmitted almost exclusively through the saliva of infected animals via bites or scratches. It is virtually 100% fatal once clinical symptoms appear — making it the deadliest infectious disease by case-fatality rate — yet it is entirely preventable with timely post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP). Globally, an estimated 59,000 people die from rabies each year, with more than 95% of deaths occurring in Africa and Asia where dog-mediated rabies remains endemic. In high-income countries, bat exposure is the dominant remaining risk. The long incubation period — typically one to three months, but sometimes up to a year — provides a critical window in which PEP, given after exposure but before symptom onset, is close to 100% effective.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 59,000 human deaths annually worldwide (WHO, 2024); endemic in more than 150 countries
keyStats.demographics: Children under 15 account for roughly 40% of human rabies deaths; highest burden in rural sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia
keyStats.avgAge: Disproportionately affects children aged 5-14 who are more likely to be bitten and less likely to report exposures
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 15 million people receive PEP worldwide each year following potential rabies exposure

definition: Rabies (ICD-10: A82.9) is a progressive, invariably fatal viral encephalomyelitis caused by RNA viruses of the genus Lyssavirus, family Rhabdoviridae. The virus travels from the wound site along peripheral nerves to the central nervous system, where it causes acute progressive encephalitis. Transmission requires introduction of infected saliva into a wound or mucous membrane — typically through a bite from a rabid animal, most commonly dogs (responsible for 99% of global human cases), but also bats, raccoons, foxes, and other mammals. The disease is classified as furious rabies (the encephalitic form, characterised by agitation, hydrophobia, and aerophobia, accounting for roughly 80% of clinical cases) and paralytic or dumb rabies (the paralytic form, resembling Guillain-Barré syndrome, accounting for the remaining 20%). Human-to-human transmission has been documented only through organ or tissue transplants. Rabies falls under infectious disease medicine, and confirmed or strongly suspected exposure requires immediate wound care and post-exposure prophylaxis in any setting worldwide.

typesClassification.description: The most common clinical presentation, occurring in about 80% of cases, marked by agitation, hyperactivity, fluctuating consciousness, hydrophobia, aerophobia, and hypersalivation; death usually occurs within days of symptom onset from cardiac or respiratory arrest.

Accounts for roughly 20% of cases; the patient develops ascending flaccid paralysis resembling Guillain-Barré syndrome, without the dramatic hydrophobia of the furious form, and may remain conscious longer before progressing to coma and death.
Wildlife-transmitted rabies, important in North America and Europe, where bats and terrestrial wildlife reservoirs such as raccoons, skunks, and foxes maintain the virus independently of dogs.
Dog-maintained rabies, the dominant transmission cycle in Africa, Asia, and Latin America, responsible for the great majority of human deaths globally.

primarySymptoms: Prodromal fever, headache, malaise, and fatigue that are indistinguishable from many common viral illnesses, typically lasting two to ten days.
Paraesthesia, pain, or itching at or near the original bite site — a specific early neurological sign that occurs in 50-80% of patients and reflects viral spread along peripheral nerves.
Hydrophobia — violent, painful spasms of the pharynx and larynx triggered by attempts to swallow water, so intense that patients refuse to drink despite severe thirst.
Aerophobia — similar spasms triggered by a gust of air across the face, highly specific for rabies and present in most furious cases.
Extreme agitation, restlessness, anxiety, and confusion alternating with periods of relative calm.
Hypersalivation, excessive lacrimation, and sweating from autonomic nervous system involvement.
Progressive ascending limb weakness and flaccid paralysis in the paralytic form, beginning in the limb that was bitten.
Diplopia, facial palsy, or other cranial nerve deficits as brainstem involvement advances.
Priapism or sexual excitement from disinhibition of spinal reflex centres in a minority of male patients.
Coma and multi-organ failure in the terminal phase, occurring within days of the neurological phase onset.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained tingling, burning, or itch at a healed animal bite wound.
Any fever or flu-like illness developing one to three months after a potential animal bite exposure.
Mild anxiety or unusual restlessness without an obvious psychiatric cause after an animal bite.
Slight difficulty swallowing or throat discomfort in the prodromal phase.
emergencySigns: Any hydrophobia or aerophobia after an animal bite — this is a medical emergency indicating clinical rabies with near-certain fatal outcome.
Progressive limb weakness after an animal bite, even without classic furious signs.
Altered consciousness or confusion after an animal bite exposure in an endemic country.
Unvaccinated person bitten by a bat in any country — immediately seek PEP assessment even if no wound is visible, as bat scratches can be imperceptible.

causes.description: Domestic dogs are the reservoir and vector for 99% of human rabies deaths worldwide; a single bite that breaks the skin from a rabid dog is sufficient for transmission if saliva containing the virus reaches muscle or nerve tissue.

Bats are the dominant risk in North America, Western Europe, and Australia; the virus can be transmitted by a bite so small it goes unnoticed, which is why any physical contact with a bat in these regions warrants PEP consideration even without a visible wound.

These terrestrial wildlife species maintain distinct lyssavirus variants in North America and Europe; bites from these animals in the wild, particularly if the animal is acting abnormally, are high-risk exposures.

causes.cause: Mucous membrane or open wound exposure to saliva
causes.description: Direct contact of infected saliva with eyes, nose, mouth, or a pre-existing skin break can theoretically transmit virus; this is lower risk than a deep bite but still warrants PEP assessment.

causes.cause: Transplanted organs or corneas from undiagnosed rabies donors
causes.description: Rare but documented clusters of transmission have occurred when organs or corneas from donors with unrecognised rabies were transplanted; this route accounts for a very small number of cases annually.
Researchers working with live lyssavirus or infected tissue samples face an occupational risk and require pre-exposure prophylaxis; aerosol exposure in high-virus environments has caused cases.

riskFactors.factor: Living in or travelling to a rabies-endemic country without pre-exposure vaccination
riskFactors.description: Sub-Saharan Africa, South and Southeast Asia, and parts of Latin America have high dog rabies burden; unvaccinated travellers and residents bear the greatest risk, particularly with prolonged rural stays.
Veterinarians, animal handlers, spelunkers (cave explorers with bat exposure), and laboratory workers handling lyssavirus have elevated occupational risk and are recommended pre-exposure prophylaxis.

Children are bitten more often, tend to play with animals, are more likely to approach rabid dogs, and are less likely to report bites — they account for roughly 40% of global deaths despite comprising a smaller fraction of bite victims.

riskFactors.factor: Bite site — head, neck, or face
riskFactors.description: Bites close to the brain produce shorter incubation periods and may give too little time for PEP to work if not administered immediately; multiple or deep bites also increase risk.

PEP is essentially 100% effective when administered promptly and completely, but delays beyond 48 hours, missing doses, or inadequate wound care reduce protection; PEP failures almost always involve incomplete regimens.
HIV infection, immunosuppressive treatment, and other immune deficiencies blunt the antibody response to PEP, requiring serological monitoring to confirm protective titres.
Vaccine and rabies immunoglobulin shortage in low- and middle-income countries means that many bite victims cannot access timely PEP; cost and distance to health facilities are major barriers.

affectedDemographics: Children under 15 in endemic regions, the highest-risk group
Rural populations in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia with frequent animal contact
Travellers to endemic countries without pre-exposure vaccination
Veterinarians, animal control officers, and cave explorers
Immunocompromised individuals who may have blunted vaccine responses
Transplant recipients of organs from unscreened donors in rare outbreak settings
diagnosisOverview: Clinical diagnosis of rabies is straightforward once the neurological phase begins — hydrophobia and aerophobia after an animal bite exposure are pathognomonic — but at that stage the disease is invariably fatal, making laboratory confirmation primarily a public health and family counselling tool. During the prodrome and early neurological phase, diagnosis is confirmed by one or more of the following: direct fluorescent antibody (DFA) or RT-PCR testing on fresh brain tissue (gold standard, post-mortem), RT-PCR on saliva, nuchal skin biopsy (testing follicular nerves around hair follicles at the nape of the neck), cerebrospinal fluid, or corneal impressions. Serum and CSF antibody testing is useful only in unvaccinated patients, as vaccinated individuals mount a response regardless of exposure. The differential diagnosis in the prodromal phase is wide and includes influenza, Guillain-Barré syndrome, other viral encephalitides, tetanus, and psychiatric illness. Any person presenting with encephalitis after a potential animal exposure should prompt urgent rabies assessment. Negri bodies — intracytoplasmic inclusions — seen on post-mortem brain histology are classic but insensitive.

diagnosticTests.test: RT-PCR on saliva and nuchal skin biopsy
diagnosticTests.purpose: The most sensitive combination for ante-mortem diagnosis, detecting viral RNA in saliva and rabies antigen in nerve fibres around hair follicles.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Saliva is collected by spitting into a sterile container; the nuchal biopsy involves removing a small skin punch at the nape of the neck under local anaesthetic. Both samples are sent to a specialist reference laboratory.

diagnosticTests.test: Direct fluorescent antibody (DFA) test on brain tissue
diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold-standard post-mortem test, using fluorescent antibodies to detect rabies antigen in brain or brainstem; performed on fresh tissue within hours of death.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed by pathology at autopsy; not applicable during life but essential for definitive confirmation and public health purposes.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum and CSF rabies virus neutralising antibody titre
diagnosticTests.purpose: In unvaccinated patients, a rising antibody titre confirms rabies; in vaccinated patients, antibody presence reflects prior vaccination rather than infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw and a lumbar puncture (spinal tap) for CSF sample; results from a reference laboratory take one to three days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Can show signal changes in the brainstem, basal ganglia, and grey matter consistent with viral encephalitis, though findings are non-specific.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient lies in the MRI scanner for 30-60 minutes; no injection is required for standard sequences, though gadolinium contrast may be added.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Grows infectious virus from saliva, CSF, or brain tissue, providing definitive confirmation though it takes several days and requires biosafety level 3 containment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Samples sent to a specialist virology reference laboratory; not available in most hospitals.

diagnosticTests.purpose: An ante-mortem test that detects antigen in corneal epithelial cells; used where nuchal biopsy is unavailable but less sensitive.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile disc is pressed briefly against the corneal surface under topical anaesthetic to collect epithelial cells for staining.

treatmentOverview: Once clinical rabies is established, no treatment has been proven to change the outcome. The Milwaukee Protocol — a regimen of ketamine-induced coma, ribavirin, and amantadine — achieved one widely publicised survival in 2004, but systematic reviews of subsequent cases show it has not produced reproducible benefit, and the patient's unusual outcome may reflect partial vaccination history. Palliative and comfort-focused care is therefore the standard of care for established clinical rabies, guided by discussions with the patient's family and ethics teams. The entire focus of rabies management is therefore prevention. Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) with three doses of cell-culture or embryo rabies vaccine on days 0, 7, and 21 or 28 is recommended for high-risk occupations and travellers. Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) is the cornerstone of prevention after any bite or scratch from a potentially rabid animal: immediate and thorough wound washing with soap and water for at least 15 minutes, followed by rabies vaccine (four or five doses depending on regimen) combined with human or equine rabies immunoglobulin (RIG) infiltrated into and around the wound on day 0. The WHO-recommended updated Essen and Zagreb PEP regimens, as well as the abridged two-site intradermal regimen, are all considered effective. PEP is close to 100% effective when completed correctly before symptom onset. Previously vaccinated individuals require only two vaccine doses without RIG.

medicalTreatments.name: Post-exposure prophylaxis — wound washing
medicalTreatments.description: Immediate and thorough washing of bite and scratch wounds with soap and water or povidone-iodine for at least 15 minutes, which physically removes virus from the wound site; this single step can reduce transmission probability by up to 90% and must precede all other PEP.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Wound washing alone reduces virus load significantly; combined with vaccine and RIG, PEP overall achieves close to 100% protection when given before symptom onset.

medicalTreatments.name: Cell-culture rabies vaccine (PEP regimen)
medicalTreatments.description: A series of intramuscular or intradermal rabies vaccine doses given on days 0, 3, 7, and 14 in previously unvaccinated persons, inducing active immunity; modern cell-culture vaccines derived from Vero cells or human diploid cells have an excellent safety and immunogenicity profile.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In combination with RIG, the full PEP course has a case-fatality rate near 0% when initiated promptly; even delayed initiation within the incubation period usually prevents disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Rabies immunoglobulin (human or equine RIG)
medicalTreatments.description: Provides immediate passive antibody protection while the vaccine induces active immunity; infiltrated into and around the wound on day 0, with any remaining dose given intramuscularly at a distant site; critical for category III exposures in unvaccinated persons.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: The combination of RIG plus vaccine has prevented rabies in essentially all correctly treated, category-III-exposed unvaccinated patients in published data.

medicalTreatments.name: Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP)
medicalTreatments.description: Three-dose vaccine series (days 0, 7, and 21 or 28) for at-risk individuals; does not eliminate the need for PEP after exposure but simplifies it to two booster doses without RIG, and extends the safe window to respond after an exposure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Seroconversion rates above 99% with modern cell-culture vaccines; protective titres persist for years, though occupational workers require periodic titre checks.

medicalTreatments.name: Palliative sedation for established clinical rabies
medicalTreatments.description: Once symptoms begin, the goal shifts to comfort: benzodiazepines, opioids, and sedatives are used to relieve the extreme distress of hydrophobia and agitation; mechanical ventilation and intensive care are offered only when there is any realistic chance of survival or pending family discussions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Does not alter mortality but substantially reduces suffering in terminal disease; median survival after symptom onset without intensive care is 7 days.

alternativeTreatments.name: Milwaukee Protocol (experimental)
alternativeTreatments.description: A combination of ketamine-induced coma, ribavirin, and amantadine developed after the 2004 Jeanna Giese case; not recommended as standard practice given failure to replicate survival in systematic reviews.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease specialist should be contacted immediately after any potential rabies exposure to determine exposure category and guide PEP decisions. In areas where equine RIG is the only available product, allergy testing and specialist oversight are important. Any patient with suspected clinical rabies needs urgent neurological and infectious disease consultation, both for comfort care planning and for public health notification.
doctorSelectionGuide: For PEP assessment, any emergency medicine physician or infectious disease specialist with access to vaccine and RIG is appropriate; speed matters far more than specialisation. For travel planning, a travel medicine clinic can assess pre-exposure prophylaxis needs. In established clinical rabies, palliative care specialists who are experienced in complex neurological decline provide the most appropriate comfort care.
preventionStrategies: Vaccinate dogs and cats — mass dog vaccination covering 70% of the canine population eliminates dog-mediated human rabies and is the most cost-effective global intervention.
Seek immediate PEP after any bite, scratch, or mucous-membrane contact with a potentially rabid animal, without waiting for laboratory confirmation of the animal.
Consider pre-exposure vaccination before travel to rabies-endemic countries, particularly for stays longer than one month, remote destinations, or children.
Do not handle bats without proper protective equipment in any country, and treat any physical contact with a bat as a potential exposure requiring PEP assessment.
Teach children to avoid stray animals and to immediately report any animal contact or bite to an adult.
Ensure PEP completion — all doses and RIG as required — without interruption once started.
lifestyleModifications: When travelling to endemic regions, know the location of facilities that stock rabies vaccine and RIG before you are bitten.
Store emergency contact numbers for the nearest travel or infectious disease clinic when in high-risk areas.
Avoid feeding, petting, or approaching stray animals in endemic countries regardless of apparent health.
When camping or caving in North America or Europe, use bat-proof tents and gloves when removing bats; any skin contact with a bat warrants medical evaluation.
Ensure that household pets are up to date on rabies vaccination, which both protects the pet and reduces risk of domestic transmission.

recommended: Adequate nutrition and hydration to support immune function during the vaccine series
No specific dietary interventions alter rabies risk or PEP effectiveness
avoid: No specific dietary restrictions are associated with rabies prevention or treatment

exerciseGuidelines: No exercise restrictions are associated with rabies or with receiving PEP. The vaccine series does not require activity modification. Individuals completing PEP can continue normal physical activity throughout.
dailyManagement: Complete all scheduled PEP vaccine doses without missing or delaying appointments.
Monitor the wound for signs of secondary bacterial infection and seek care promptly if redness, swelling, or pus develops.
Keep a written record of each vaccine dose received, date, and administering facility in case the course must be continued elsewhere.
Report any neurological symptoms — tingling at the wound site, headache, or fever — immediately during or after PEP.
Notify local public health authorities of the exposure so the responsible animal can be tested or quarantined.
prognosis: Clinical rabies has a case-fatality rate approaching 100% in unvaccinated patients who develop symptoms — it is the most lethal infectious disease known to medicine by this measure. Among the handful of documented survivors with confirmed clinical rabies who received no or partial prior vaccination, nearly all had significant neurological sequelae. In contrast, the prognosis with timely and complete PEP before symptom onset is excellent, with failure rates less than 0.1% in published case series; virtually all failures involved incomplete wound care, delayed initiation, missed doses, or improper RIG administration. Global elimination of dog-mediated rabies through mass canine vaccination is considered achievable and is a WHO target for 2030.
recoveryTimeline: Post-exposure prophylaxis is typically completed over 14-28 days depending on regimen. Local wound reactions and mild systemic side effects from the vaccine (arm soreness, low-grade fever) resolve within 24-72 hours of each dose. Individuals who receive complete PEP and remain symptom-free have no ongoing health implications. Clinical rabies, once established, has a neurological phase lasting two to ten days in the furious form before coma and death; the paralytic form may extend to one to three weeks. Rare reported survivors required months of intensive rehabilitation.
complications: Encephalitis progressing to coma and death — the defining outcome of established clinical disease in virtually all unprotected cases.
Respiratory failure — the final common pathway in furious rabies, caused by pharyngeal and respiratory muscle spasm together with brainstem involvement.
Cardiac arrhythmias and myocarditis — autonomic dysfunction causes life-threatening rhythm abnormalities in the terminal phase.
Secondary bacterial infection of the original bite wound — cellulitis, abscess, or sepsis can develop independently of rabies risk and require antibiotic treatment.
Severe allergic reaction to equine RIG — anaphylaxis occurs in 0.1-1% of patients given equine product and requires immediate epinephrine treatment; skin testing is recommended before administration.
Neurological sequelae in rare survivors — the few documented clinical survivors who recovered have had varying degrees of cognitive, motor, and behavioural impairment.

supportResources.name: WHO — Rabies fact sheet
supportResources.description: Comprehensive global overview of rabies epidemiology, PEP guidelines, and elimination targets from the World Health Organization.

supportResources.name: CDC — Rabies
supportResources.description: U.S.-focused guidance on rabies prevention, animal exposure assessment, PEP, and pre-exposure vaccination from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: NHS — Rabies
supportResources.description: Plain-English overview for travellers and UK residents on recognising exposure risk and accessing PEP.

sources.title: WHO Expert Consultation on Rabies, Third Report. WHO Technical Report Series No. 1012 (2018)

confusedWithConditions.name: Viral encephalitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Viral encephalitis from other causes presents with fever, confusion, and seizures without the pathognomonic hydrophobia and aerophobia of rabies; the distinction is critical because other viral encephalitides may be treatable.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tetanus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tetanus causes trismus (lockjaw) and generalised muscle spasms triggered by sensory stimuli but does not produce hydrophobia or aerophobia, and it typically follows a wound contaminated with soil rather than an animal bite.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bacterial meningitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bacterial meningitis features meningismus, photophobia, and purpuric rash in meningococcal disease; it lacks the bite history and the specific autonomic signs of rabies.

confusedWithConditions.name: Herpes simplex encephalitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Herpes simplex encephalitis predominantly affects the temporal lobes, presenting with focal seizures and personality change; it is treatable with aciclovir, unlike rabies, and the absence of an animal bite exposure is an important distinguishing feature.

relatedConditions.name: Sylvatic rabies
relatedConditions.relevance: Wildlife-maintained rabies, the dominant form in North America and Europe, sharing the same lyssavirus pathogen and identical PEP principles.

relatedConditions.name: Urban rabies
relatedConditions.relevance: Dog-maintained rabies, responsible for 99% of human deaths globally, differing from sylvatic rabies only in its animal reservoir and geographic distribution.

relatedConditions.name: Botulism
relatedConditions.relevance: Botulism enters the differential for descending paralysis; the paralytic form of rabies can mimic its progressive weakness, though the exposure history and cranial nerve pattern differ.

relatedConditions.name: Herpes simplex encephalitis
relatedConditions.relevance: An important differential diagnosis for acute viral encephalitis with behavioural change; identification is urgent as HSV encephalitis is treatable.

faqs.question: Can rabies be cured once symptoms start?
faqs.answer: No. Once clinical rabies begins, it is almost universally fatal. No treatment has been shown to reliably change the outcome. The very rare documented survivors typically had partial prior vaccination. All efforts must focus on prevention through post-exposure prophylaxis before symptoms appear.

faqs.question: What should I do immediately after being bitten by an animal?
faqs.answer: Wash the wound thoroughly with soap and water for at least 15 minutes, then apply an antiseptic such as iodine solution. Seek medical attention immediately — do not wait to see if the animal becomes ill. A doctor will assess your exposure category and start post-exposure prophylaxis if needed.

faqs.question: How long after a bite do I have to get rabies PEP?
faqs.answer: There is no hard deadline, but earlier is better. PEP is highly effective when started promptly and completed in full before symptoms appear. The incubation period is usually one to three months, so even delayed presentation warrants assessment. Do not wait — seek care on the same day as the exposure.

faqs.question: Is rabies always transmitted by a bite?
faqs.answer: Almost always, yes. Transmission requires infected saliva to enter broken skin or a mucous membrane. A bite is by far the most common route. Scratches that break the skin, licks on open wounds, and very rarely, corneal or organ transplants from undiagnosed donors are other documented routes. Casual contact, petting an animal, or exposure to blood does not transmit rabies.

faqs.question: Do I need rabies shots if a vaccinated dog bites me?
faqs.answer: The dog's vaccine status, the exposure circumstances, and local epidemiology all factor into the decision. In countries where dog rabies is endemic, a vaccinated dog bite still warrants medical evaluation for PEP, because vaccine records may be unreliable. In rabies-free countries, a confirmed up-to-date vaccination and a dog that remains healthy during a 10-day observation period generally means PEP is not required. Always consult a doctor.

faqs.question: How long is the incubation period for rabies?
faqs.answer: The most common incubation period is one to three months, but it can range from days to over a year. Shorter incubations occur with bites close to the brain — face, neck, hands — or with multiple deep wounds. Longer incubations are more common with minor extremity wounds. This variability is why PEP is recommended even weeks or months after a bite.

faqs.question: Can I get rabies from a bat without being bitten?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bat bites can be so small they go unnoticed — in North America, any physical contact with a bat (touching it, waking up to find one in the room) should be treated as a potential exposure and reported to a doctor for PEP assessment. Aerosol exposure in caves with millions of bats has caused cases in rare instances.

faqs.question: What is post-exposure prophylaxis for rabies?
faqs.answer: Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) consists of thorough wound washing followed by a series of rabies vaccine injections — usually four doses over 14 days. In unvaccinated people with significant exposures, rabies immunoglobulin is also injected into and around the wound on the first day. When given correctly and completely, PEP is close to 100% effective.

faqs.question: Is rabies present in the UK?
faqs.answer: Terrestrial animal rabies was eliminated from the UK in 1902. Bat lyssavirus (European bat lyssavirus) does circulate in British bat populations and has caused one human death. There is no risk from dogs, foxes, or other land animals in the UK, but handling bats without protective equipment is not recommended.

faqs.question: What animals carry rabies?
faqs.answer: Any mammal can carry rabies. Dogs are responsible for 99% of human deaths globally. In North America, bats, raccoons, skunks, and foxes are the main wildlife reservoirs. In Europe, foxes and bats are the primary wildlife reservoirs. Rabbits, squirrels, and rodents are rarely infected and are not considered significant vectors. Birds, reptiles, and fish cannot transmit rabies.

faqs.question: How is rabies diagnosed?
faqs.answer: During life, the most sensitive tests are RT-PCR on saliva and a nuchal skin biopsy detecting the virus in nerve fibres around hair follicles. Hydrophobia and aerophobia after an animal bite are clinically diagnostic in the right epidemiological setting. The gold-standard confirmation is direct fluorescent antibody testing on brain tissue, which is usually performed post-mortem.

faqs.question: Can children receive the rabies vaccine?
faqs.answer: Yes. Rabies vaccine is safe for all ages including infants, and children in endemic regions should receive PEP for any potential exposure without delay. Pre-exposure prophylaxis is recommended for children travelling to or living in endemic areas where prompt medical access is limited.

faqs.question: Is there a preventive vaccine for rabies?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pre-exposure prophylaxis with three vaccine doses on days 0, 7, and 21 or 28 is recommended for people at high risk — travellers to endemic regions, vets, animal handlers, and laboratory workers. It does not eliminate the need for PEP after an exposure but simplifies the course to two boosters without immunoglobulin.

faqs.question: What does hydrophobia in rabies look like?
faqs.answer: Hydrophobia is not simply a fear of water — it is a violent, painful spasm of the throat and breathing muscles triggered by the sight, sound, or thought of swallowing water. The spasms are so severe and frightening that patients refuse to drink despite extreme thirst. It is caused by virus-induced hypersensitivity of brainstem swallowing reflex circuits and is highly specific for rabies.

faqs.question: Does rabies PEP work if started late?
faqs.answer: PEP is generally effective at any time during the incubation period, before symptoms begin. The incubation period is typically weeks to months, providing a window. There is no absolute deadline, but earlier start produces higher confidence. Once clinical symptoms appear, PEP cannot reverse the disease.

faqs.question: How many doses of vaccine does PEP require?
faqs.answer: For a previously unvaccinated person, the standard WHO-recommended PEP is four intramuscular doses on days 0, 3, 7, and 14, combined with rabies immunoglobulin on day 0. A five-dose Essen regimen (days 0, 3, 7, 14, 28) is used in some countries. Previously vaccinated individuals need only two doses without immunoglobulin.

faqs.question: What is the difference between furious and paralytic rabies?
faqs.answer: Furious rabies, occurring in about 80% of cases, is marked by agitation, hyperactivity, hydrophobia, and aerophobia. Paralytic rabies, in about 20%, presents as progressive limb weakness resembling Guillain-Barré syndrome without dramatic hydrophobia. Both forms are fatal without prior vaccination, but paralytic rabies may be misdiagnosed and progress unrecognised.

faqs.question: Can rabies be transmitted person to person?
faqs.answer: Ordinary contact — living with, nursing, or being near someone with rabies — does not transmit the disease. The only documented human-to-human transmission has been through transplanted organs or corneas from donors who were not recognised as having rabies at the time of death. Medical staff caring for rabies patients should use standard barrier precautions but do not require PEP.

faqs.question: How much does rabies vaccine cost?
faqs.answer: Costs vary widely by country and product. In the United States, a full PEP course including immunoglobulin can cost several thousand dollars without insurance. In many endemic low-income countries, intradermal rabies vaccine regimens reduce per-patient costs substantially, but access and supply remain challenges. WHO promotes the cheaper intradermal route to expand coverage.

faqs.question: What is the global burden of rabies?
faqs.answer: Rabies kills an estimated 59,000 people a year, with over 95% of deaths in Africa and Asia. Dogs are responsible for 99% of these deaths. Approximately 15 million people receive PEP each year. Without dog vaccination campaigns, the burden would be far higher; mass canine vaccination has already eliminated dog-mediated rabies in most of Latin America and Europe.

faqs.question: When should I seek emergency care for a possible rabies exposure?
faqs.answer: Go to an emergency department or urgent care the same day as any bite or scratch from a potentially rabid animal, any contact with a bat, or any mucous-membrane exposure to saliva from a suspected rabid animal. Do not wait for the animal to show symptoms, as not all infected animals appear sick. Wound washing at home comes first, then immediate medical assessment.

metaDescription: Rabies is nearly 100% fatal once symptoms appear but fully preventable with prompt post-exposure prophylaxis. Learn symptoms, PEP, and prevention. Doctor-reviewed.
